lines.text: The bus doors hiss open, and Ridgeview High swallows you whole — noise, lockers, a thousand people who already know exactly where they're going.
Okay. Cool. Normal. Everyone else got the map memorized in the womb, apparently.
(checking the crumpled paper) Room 114. Room 114. It's around here. Somewhere. Probably.
A knot of upperclassmen breezes past, laughing at something that has nothing to do with you. It still manages to feel like it does.
First days should be illegal. Like, constitutionally.
The locker groans, sticks, then gives all at once — binders exploding across the tile like it's been waiting all week to do this.
A cluster of students passing by slows down. A couple of them laugh — not cruel, exactly, just loud, the way laughing at someone else's bad luck is always a little louder than it needs to be.
Bro. BRO. Your whole binder just did a base jump.

At the edge of the crowd, a girl with a sketchbook under one arm — Reyna — stops walking. She glances at the mess like she wants to help, then at the crowd, like she's doing math on whether it's worth it.
Everyone's looking. Okay. Okay okay okay. Pick a face and wear it.
Marcus's smirk breaks into an actual laugh — the surprised kind, like it got past him before he could file it away.
Okay that's — okay that's actually kind of iconic, ngl. 'Free-range binders.' I'm stealing that.
You're funny. Sit with us later, yeah?
A couple of the kids around him chuckle too, already re-slotting you into a different category than 'new kid who fell apart in the hallway.'
That felt good. Weirdly good. Like passing a test I didn't know I signed up for.
(quieter) ...cool. Cool cool cool.
Face burning, you drop to your knees and start scooping binders into a stack that will never look organized again.
A pair of sneakers stops beside you. Reyna crouches down without a word and starts stacking papers into neat, exact corners.
(quiet) You don't have to —
I know.
Locker fifty-two used to eat people's stuff too. It's not just you.
She taps the finished stack once, like she's testing that it'll hold, and gives you a small, real smile before slipping back into the hallway current — like she was never there.
Huh. Okay. That's... something.
What's so funny?
The hallway dips into a beat of surprised quiet. A couple of the laughing kids trade a look and suddenly get very interested in their phones.
Whoa. Okay. Didn't say anything, new kid.
lines.emotion: eyebrow raised, more curious than offended

lines.text: Great. Fantastic start. Nothing says 'please don't talk to me' like snapping at the first person you meet.
Marcus gives you a considering look — not unkind, just filing you away as something he hasn't figured out yet — and moves on.
(muttering) ...whatever. It's whatever.
The cafeteria hits you like a wall of sound — trays clattering, a hundred conversations at once, nobody looking lost except you.
Marcus's table is easy to spot — loudest corner, most laughing, him holding court like it's a full-time job.
Near the window, Reyna sits alone, sketchbook open, pencil moving in short, sure strokes, like she isn't waiting for anyone.
Closer to the vending machines, Devon — the kid from the locker disaster — props a paperback against his tray, one earbud in, completely unbothered.
Every table is basically a life decision at this point. No pressure.
Marcus slides over to make room like you've been sitting here all year.
Yo — locker girl! I mean — locker person. We're workshopping the nickname.
Please don't.
See, that's the energy. Okay. Sit, sit.
The table absorbs you fast — someone's mid-story about a gym teacher, someone else is ranking cafeteria pizza by 'structural integrity.'
(glancing across the room, chin-lift) ...anyway, don't sit near sketchbook girl over there, she'll just draw you and never talk to you. Weird vibe.
A couple kids laugh. It's quick, almost nothing — the kind of joke that costs the person who says it absolutely nothing at all.
That landed weird. Nobody else seemed to notice. Or they noticed and decided not to.
Anyway — you doing the talent show thing? No cap, half of us are just doing it for extra credit vibes.
Reyna looks up when your tray hits the table, surprise flickering across her face before she catches it.
Oh. Hi. Um — you can sit. If you want. I mean, you're already sitting, so.
(small smile) Smooth save.
I've been told.
It's kind of dumb, but — I've been working on this since sixth grade. It has an actual plot now. Which is either really cool or a cry for help.
Can I see?
She pauses like she's deciding something, then nods once and slides it fully toward you.
It's fine. I eat lunch here most days. Nobody bothers you, and you can actually hear yourself think.
(quieter, testing the words) ...it's usually just me, though. So. This is different.
You pick an empty table in the middle distance — close enough to see everything, far enough to be part of nothing.
Marcus's table sounds like a party that started without an invitation list. Reyna's corner is just... quiet. Steady.
Devon flips a page of his paperback without looking up once, completely unbothered by being alone.
Nobody's looking at me over here. That's the point. That's also, somehow, the problem.
The noise of the cafeteria fills in around your silence like water finding the shape of a container.
Just watch today. There's always tomorrow to actually show up.
Room 114 smells like dry-erase markers and a radiator that runs a little too warm.
Okay, okay, okay — settle in, settle in. Welcome to English, or as I like to call it, the best fifty minutes of your day. No pressure.
Paired project, due Friday. You choose your partners — not permanent marriages, people. You can survive four days.
The room shuffles into motion. Marcus twists around in his seat, already waving you over with a grin.
Yo — new kid. Partner up. I promise I'm a genius when it counts.
A few seats over, Reyna glances up, pen paused over her notebook — hope flickering across her face before she looks back down, like she's already expecting the answer.
Devon doesn't look up at all — just keeps doodling in the margin, pencil tapping against his knuckles, ready to be picked by whoever. Or nobody.
Okay. This one actually matters.
Marcus spins his chair backward and drapes his arms over the backrest like the project is a minor inconvenience.
Okay so — weekend. Did you see the game? No? Doesn't matter, it was crazy.
(glancing at the blank page) ...we do have a project due Friday.
Right, right. Project. Yeah.
lines.action: taps a rhythm on the desk, thinking

lines.text: His eyes land on a rough draft sitting open on the desk behind you — Reyna's, left there while she's at the sharpener.
She's not even using it right now. Thirty seconds, nobody has to know.
That's... not nothing. That's actually kind of a big something.
You flip the draft open before you can really think about it — her handwriting dense and precise, better than it has any right to be for a first draft.
(muttering) ...okay, this line about 'monsters are just metaphors that got tired of waiting' is actually really good.
Perfect. Love it. Ship it.
It works fast. Too fast. The outline comes together in minutes instead of the hour it should've taken.
(quiet) That's not — that wasn't mine to take.
Bro, we are DONE. Best twenty minutes of my life.
I mean — I'd rather just come up with our own thing. Feels weird otherwise.
Fine. Your funeral.
...what've you got, then?
You toss out a rough idea. Marcus actually leans forward, considering it like it's the first interesting thing that's happened all period.
Okay wait — what if the monster's actually just, like, misunderstood homework. No cap, that's kind of funny.
(small smile) ...that's actually not bad.
It's thin, and it's a little chaotic, and it's entirely, unglamorously yours.
Not the easy way. But it's ours.
Between the bell's echo and the shuffle of the next class, you catch Reyna near the sharpener.
(quiet) Hey — Marcus looked at your draft. Just so you know.
Reyna goes still for half a second, jaw tightening, before she nods once and pulls the notebook tighter against her chest.
Great. Cool. Love that.
(quieter, almost to herself) I'll be ready this time.
That's not exactly making it right. But it's something.
Reyna blinks like she wasn't expecting to actually be picked.
Oh. Okay. Um — hi.
Hi. So, what've you got? Feels like you already have ideas.
Something shifts in her face — surprise, then something warmer she tries to tamp back down.
It's kind of dumb, but — what if the monster in the story isn't the villain. What if it's just... misfiled. Like a paperwork error nobody wants to fix.
That's actually really good.
You don't have to say that.
lines.action: ducking her head, hiding a real smile

lines.text: (quieter) ...but thanks. Usually people just pick the loudest idea in the group and move on.
She flips to a fresh page, already sketching, like your attention unlocked something she'd been saving.
Devon glances up, mildly surprised, like he'd already resigned himself to being picked last. Or not at all.
(a beat late) ...oh. Okay. Cool.
So. Any ideas?
I mean, I could draw it. I'm not saying it'd be good. I'm saying it would exist, which is a low bar, but it's a bar.
(laughs) I'll allow it.
He flips his notebook around — a fully inked monster mid-margin, tiny and detailed and clearly not his first.
Oh, you actually want the whole backstory? Okay. Buckle up. This is gonna take way longer than the assignment requires.
Somehow, twenty minutes later, you've got an outline, three inside jokes, and the vague sense that this project might actually be fun.
Reyna stands at the front, sketchbook propped against the whiteboard tray, voice steady but quiet.
So — the monster isn't the villain. It's more like... a paperwork error. Something misfiled that everyone's scared of just because nobody bothered to relabel it.
A beat of silence while the class processes it — and then, from Marcus's table, a laugh that's too loud to be about anything else.
(not quite under his breath, to his table) ...weird. That's a weird one.
A few kids snicker. Reyna's shoulders pull in half an inch, her eyes dropping to the sketchbook like she can disappear into it.
(still writing on the board, not yet looking up) Okay, hang on, who's next, we've got —
She hasn't noticed. Not yet. The room is very quiet, and very much waiting to see what you do.
Reyna's presenting now, and the words coming out of her mouth are uncomfortably, exactly the ones you and Marcus put on the board twenty minutes ago.
(reading from her notes) ...so the monster's basically a metaphor that got tired of waiting to be understood —
A couple of heads turn between her and your poster. Someone whispers something that isn't quiet enough.
(faltering, eyes flicking up to your board) ...that's — wait.
(glancing between the two projects, frowning slightly) Huh. That's — okay, hang on, let's back up a second.
The room waits. Reyna doesn't look at you, but somehow that's louder than if she had.
Actually, I don't think it's weird. I think it's kind of the best idea in the room.
The room goes quiet in a different way this time — not laughing-quiet, waiting-quiet.
Whoa, chill, it's not that deep, it's just a project.
Then it shouldn't be a big deal to let her finish.
A couple of classmates nod, murmuring agreement. Marcus opens his mouth, then just shrugs, deflating a little.
(quieter, losing the performance) ...whatever. It's whatever.
(quiet, almost to herself) ...you didn't have to do that.
I know.
(small, real) ...thanks.
You open your mouth. Nothing comes out. The laughter fades on its own, the way these things do, and Ms. Alvarez finally looks up.
Okay — eyes up here, let's keep it moving, yeah?
Reyna sits back down without a word, sketchbook clutched a little tighter than before.
(already leaning to his table, grinning about something else) Bro, okay, but next presentation better top that —
Say something. Say anything.
(quieter) ...it's not my fight.
The bell hasn't even rung yet, and the silence you didn't fill is already sitting heavy in your chest.
Okay, but 'misfiled monster' is honestly a better concept than half the stuff on my Netflix list.
A ripple of actual laughter — the good kind — moves through the room, and the mocking laughter dissolves into it, redirected without anyone quite noticing how.
...yeah, okay, fair.
(finally looking up) Alright — keep going, Reyna, you've got the room.
Later, while everyone's packing up, you lean toward Reyna's desk.
(quiet) For what it's worth, I really did like it.
(surprised) ...oh.
Thanks. That actually — yeah. Thanks.
...actually, that idea — the metaphor one — that was Reyna's. From her draft. Not mine.
The room shifts, murmurs rippling through like a current changing direction.
It's not — it's not a big deal, man, chill —
Marcus Webb. We'll talk after.
(quiet) ...you didn't have to say that.
I know. I wanted to.
Something like relief moves across her face, and a couple of classmates actually murmur, impressed, before Ms. Alvarez waves the class back to order.
You don't say anything. The seconds stretch out, and then Marcus fills them the way he fills everything.
I mean, great minds, right? Must've just clicked the same way.
A couple of kids laugh, and just like that, the room moves on. Ms. Alvarez frowns but lets it go, already calling the next presenter.
Reyna doesn't argue. She just sits down, and she doesn't look up again for the rest of class.
Say something. It's not too late.
(quieter) ...it's fine. It's whatever. It's over now.
It doesn't feel over. Not even a little.
You catch Ms. Alvarez at her desk while the room empties out.
(quiet) Hey — um. That idea Marcus and I presented? It was actually Reyna's. From her draft.
Ms. Alvarez pauses, pen tapping once against her clipboard, and studies you for a second.
...okay. Thank you for telling me. That took something.
The next day, she makes a point of holding up Reyna's original draft in front of the class, praising the line about monsters and misfiled paperwork like she just noticed it for the first time.
Reyna never quite knows why. But she sits a little straighter after that, and doesn't duck her head quite as fast when someone looks at her sketchbook.
The activities board outside the office is covered in overlapping flyers, but the talent show sign-up sheet is the one everyone's crowding around.
Marcus's crowd has already claimed half the slots — a dance thing, a 'meme review,' something involving a kazoo that nobody's questioning.
Off to the side, Reyna hovers near the sheet, pen uncapped, clearly rereading the same line for the third time.
(not quite looking at you) ...there's an art category. Nobody really signs up for it.
Are you going to?
I don't know. Probably not. It's kind of — a lot, to put your stuff up where people can just... look at it.
She doesn't move. Neither, for a second, do you.
Okay. What's actually yours on this list.
You uncap the pen and write your name down next to the thing you actually, unglamorously love — no group, no cushion, just you and the thing.
Reyna watches you do it, something shifting in her face.
(quiet) ...okay.
She steps up and signs her name under the art category, fast, like if she thinks about it too long she won't.
(almost surprised at herself) There. Now it's real. Or, like, paper-real. Which counts.
(smiling) It counts.
(ducking her head, hiding a real smile) ...cool. Cool cool cool.
You write your name under the activity Marcus's crowd already claimed — easier this way, you tell yourself, and it's not even really a lie.
Yes! Okay, that's — okay, we're gonna be so good, no cap. Welcome aboard, officially.
It feels good, the way easy things feel good — warm, immediate, uncomplicated.
(quieter) ...it's fine. It's just a sign-up sheet.
Somewhere behind you, the art category sits with exactly one name on it, and it isn't yours.
You read the sheet twice. Your pen doesn't touch it. Somewhere in the second read-through, you already know you're not going to.
Safer this way. Nobody signs up to fail in front of a crowd.
You cap the pen, step back, and let the hallway do what hallways do at the end of the day — empty out, slow and unremarkable, around you.
(quieter) ...maybe tomorrow.
The sheet stays exactly the same, whether or not you were ever standing in front of it.
The bus stop empties out slow, backpacks and bad first-day stories trading hands.
(sitting down next to you, like it's already decided) So. That was a day.
That was a day.
Neither of you rushes to fill the quiet after that — which, somehow, doesn't feel awkward at all.
The bus pulls in as your phone buzzes — a meme from a number you just saved an hour ago.
(reading it) ...huh.
It's a good feeling, the kind that comes with a group chat already forming around you before you've even left the parking lot.
The bus stop is quiet, mostly empty, the day already folding itself into memory.
Nobody laughed at you today. Nobody really noticed you either. Both of those are true at the same time.

a.text: (loudly, to the hallway) Cool, cool, love that for me. Free-range binders — very trendy this season.

b.text: (under his breath, scrambling) Nope. Nope, we're not doing this. Just — gather, go, disappear.

a.text: You angle your tray toward the loudest table in the room and hope your face looks more confident than you feel.

b.text: You cross the cafeteria toward the quiet corner table, tray suddenly feeling very loud in your hands.

c.text: You pick a table with no claim on it at all, and set your tray down like it's no big deal.

b.text: Hey — you want to team up?

c.text: (sliding into the seat next to him) Looks like it's you and me.

options.text: Go along with it — use her draft
Refuse and build your own idea
Play along for now, warn her after

b.text: Nah. Let's just — figure out our own thing.

options.text: Stay quiet and let it pass
Redirect the room, then support her quietly

a.text: (standing a little straighter) Yeah, no — I'm saying something.

b.text: Not your fight. Not your fight.

options.text: Stay quiet and let Marcus take credit
Tell Ms. Alvarez privately after class

b.text: Don't look at her. Don't look at her.

c.text: Not now. After. I'll fix it after.

options.text: Sign up for what you actually love
Sign up for the popular activity

a.text: (pen already moving) Alright. This one, then.

lines.text: (tapping her sketchbook) I actually signed up for the art thing. Talent show. Which is either really brave or a huge mistake.
It's not a mistake.
(small, real smile) ...yeah. Okay. Maybe not.
Same table tomorrow?
(like she's testing the words) ...yeah. Same table.
(text) yo. tomorrow. sit with us again. you're in, officially, no cap 🔥
In. That's the word he used. In.
(quieter) ...so why does it feel like I left something outside the door.
The sign-up sheet is probably still sitting there, blank next to the space where your name could've gone.
(quieter) ...there's always tomorrow. That's what you keep telling yourself, anyway.

voiceNotes: Jordan (player) runs on deflective humor when nervous and goes quiet/slow when something actually lands emotionally — her INTERNAL lines should almost always undercut or second-guess the previous beat. Marcus's bravado ('no cap,' finger guns, hype-man narration) is a performance that cracks into short, clipped, quieter lines the instant he's genuinely called out ('...whatever. It's whatever.') — this collapse is his only real tell. Reyna stays clipped and self-undercutting ('It's kind of dumb, but—') until she trusts someone, then over-explains with weirdly precise vocabulary about the thing she loves (monsters, metaphors, misfiled paperwork). She almost never laughs out loud — track the half-smile/ducked head instead. Devon's humor lands a beat late and completely deadpan; he never signals a joke is coming. Ms. Alvarez's authority is genuinely warm but always a little overwhelmed — she almost always notices the real problem a beat too late, and that lateness is intentional characterization, not a plot hole. Mirroring device: player's 'cool cool cool' and Marcus's 'it's whatever' are meant to occasionally cross-contaminate other characters' speech (e.g., Reyna picking up 'cool cool cool' in the authentic ending) as a subtle marker of influence/connection.

alternatives: Bro. That's not even sad, that's just chaos.
Okay that binder had NO business doing that.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps Marcus's hype-man narration voice but offers softer/harder options depending on how mean vs. amused the scene should read.

alternatives: I mean... I don't think it's weird. I think it's actually kind of the best idea we've heard.
Hold on — that's not weird, that's just good, and you all know it.
alternativeLines.reasoning: First alternative adds a hesitation beat consistent with Jordan's conflict-avoidant trait even while she's being brave; second is more openly confrontational if a punchier read is wanted.

alternatives: (quiet) Okay. It's on the sheet now. That's — a lot, actually.
(exhaling) There. Can't take it back now.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Reyna's self-effacing register could tip toward more visible anxiety instead of dry humor, depending on how vulnerable this beat should feel.
